details.text: Derivative: الْبُرُوجِ is the plural of the word بُرْج (Burj) which refers to a tall building like a tower or castle. Anything that is high and you have look up to see is a بَرْج (Burj). بُروج (Burooj) is also used to refer to large stars and prior to Islam different constellations of stars were referred to as بُروج (Burooj). The use of بُروج (Burooj) which can refer to towers and castles which have military significance can imply that they are where the garrisons of Angels are stationed. The origin of بَرَجَ means to be raised high and to be made manifest which can refer to the most brilliant stars in the sky.

Derivative: فَتَنَ – فِتْنَة in origin refers to a meticulous process of purification which was used to describe the process of purifying and testing the purity of gold through extreme heat. It means to put to the test or expose to a test or trial. It is consistently used in the Qur’an when the test is something that can take someone away from Islam through oppression, fear or temptation. Here it is used in reference to those who torture and burn the believers to take them away from their belief.

Derivative: إِنَّ can be translated by a word such as “Indeed” or “Verily” for the purpose of emphasis however it also implies the "removal of doubt" and as such can be translated as: "There is no doubt (that)".
Derivative: جَهَنَّمَ has a Persian origin from the Persian word Jahnam which means a torture chamber. جَهَنَّمَ is the name for hellfire and includes all the punishments that occur in it.

- In Arabic the verb implies something that is temporary whilst a noun implies something permanent. The use of الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ “The male believers and female believers” indicates the permanence and firmness of their Iman. In line with this it is observed that the terms of الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ is only used in the Qur’an in commendation and not criticism or rebuke in contrast to يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا “O you who have believed”.
Derivative: جَنَّاتٌ is the plural of جَنَّة (Jannah) and it refers to lush gardens which are completely covered in beauty.
Derivative: بَطْشَ refers to the act of grabbing or seizing hold of someone with speed, force and violence whereby they are unable to escape from it.
Derivative: شَدِيدٌ comes from شَدَّ - شِدَّة which refers to pulling something tight and taut. It means something that is very intense or severe.
Derivative: الْوَدُودُ is He who loves intensely with great affection.

Derivative: الْمَجِيدُ comes from مَجَد - يَمْجُدُ – مَجْد and مَجَادَة which indicates to nobility, grandeur and glory. الْمَجِيدُ is the One who is of the highest nobility, grandeur, glorification and distinction.
Derivative: فَعَّالٌ is the مُبالَغَة (emphasised and accentuated) form of فَاعِل (the doer) and it means to do over and over again i.e., constantly and continuously.

Derivative: الْجُنُودِ means soldiers and refers to those who form a well-armed and organised army. الْجُنُودِ has also been said to refer more generally to the masses of the people in reference to those who denied and opposed their Prophets. It implies that they were a powerful force.
Derivative: حَدِيثُ refers to speech that is new or recent. When old news is forgotten and then reminded of again as if it is new, it is called حَدِيث (Hadeeth).

Derivative: تَكْذِيب means to persistently lie against or deny something. The statement فِي (in) تَكْذِيبٍ means that they are in a state of تَكْذِيبٍ i.e., immersed in a state of persistent lying against and denial of the truth.
Derivative: الْمَوْعُودِ means “promised” and is from the word وَعَدَ – يَعِدُ – وَعْدًا.

Derivative: وَرَاء means behind but is from the words in Arabic that can carry opposite meanings at the same time i.e., behind as well as in front. Here, due to the context of being encompassed, engulfed and surrounded (مُحِيطٌ - Muheet), it means in front and behind.

وَشَاهِدٍ وَمَشْهُودٍ

- Both words are derived from شَهِدَ – يَشْهَدُ – شَهَادَة which means to bear witness and to be present and before something. شَاهِد is a “witness” and مَشْهُودٍ is that which is “witnessed”. Numerous interpretations have been provided for what the شَاهِد and مَشْهُودٍ are specifically referring to here as they have been referred to in so many realities mentioned in the Qur’an and related to the Deen. One opinion which was prevalent amongst the Sahabah originated from a Mursal narration that stated that شَاهِد refers to the day of Jumu’ah (Friday) and that مَشْهُودٍ refers to the day of ‘Arafah.

Derivative: أَصْحَابُ is the plural of صاحِب (companion) which includes the meanings of closeness and affiliation i.e., it refers to someone who was known closely and for a long period of time. The term أَصْحَابُ here in conjunction with الْأُخْدُودِ (the trench) implies that they have become known due to their close affiliation to the incident of the trench i.e., after the incident they became known by association as أَصْحَابُ (the people/companions) of الْأُخْدُودِ (the trench).

Derivative: قُتِلَ is in the past passive voice (مَبْنِي عَلَى المجْهُول) tense. قُتِلَ literally means “he was killed” but in Arabic it can be used to mean “to curse” i.e., “May someone be cursed or damned”.
Derivative: الْأُخْدُودِ refers to a large rectangular shaped ditch dug into the earth. It is from خَدَّ which means to dig the ground.
Derivative: قُعُودٌ means to “be sitting” and implies sitting for a while in contrast to جُلوُس that does not imply staying for a while in the sitting position.

Derivative: يَفْعَلُونَ “they were doing” is used and differs from يَعْمَلُونَ “they were doing” in that يَفْعَلُونَ implies a lack of thought associated with the action. In this context its usage can imply their indifference to what they were doing i.e., torturing and killing the believers.
Derivative: شُهُودٌ can mean “witnesses” as the plural of شَاهِد (a witness) or شُهُودٌ can be the Masdar and mean “Witnessing” i.e., in attendance and watching

Derivative: نَقَمُوا from نَقَمَ – نِقْمَةً. نِقْمَةً (Niqmah) refers to a source of hatred and resentment. نَقَمُوا refers to that which causes resentment and hatred. It can also refer to the impulse to take revenge against someone for the offense they believe they have caused against them.
Derivative: يُؤْمِنُوا means “they believe” and the use of the present tense alludes to continuation i.e., they continued to believe and held on to their belief.
Derivative: الْعَزِيزِ comes from عِزَّة (‘Izzah) which contains the meaning of authority. The belief in الْعَزِيزِ (Al-‘Azeez) implies acknowledgement of the Allah’s authority over everything.

Derivative: الْحَمِيدِ comes from الحَمْد (Al-Hamd) which implies two matters: شُكْر (Shukr - gratitude) and ثَنَاء (Thanaa’ – Praise or extolling). الْحَمِيدِ (Al-Hameed) means the One who is worthy of praise and gratitude.
Derivative: مُلْكُ (Mulk) means sovereignty i.e., the complete and absolute command and authority. Sovereignty implies that nobody else can question what the possessor of sovereignty does.
- By bringing لَهُ “to Him” forward in the sentence structure, it means that the dominion and sovereignty (مُلْكُ) of the heavens and the earth exclusively belongs to Him.

- عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ “Upon every single thing” is brought forward in the sentence structure and precedes شَهِيدٌ (To be a witness) to add emphasis to the fact that Allah is witness “to every single thing without exception”.
